title: Wormhole: from networks to Plato
subtitle: Follow concept learning until a wormhole leaps to philosophy.

steps.title: Cognition from connections
steps.body: Connectionism: the idea that thinking emerges from networks of simple units, each doing almost nothing alone. "Parallel Distributed Processing" is its banner. Let's follow this idea — how a network comes to know things — and see where it leads.

steps.title: Learning what things mean
steps.body: If cognition is a network, where does MEANING come from? We follow a citation to "Semantic Cognition" — how such a network gradually comes to represent concepts: what a robin is, how it differs from a rose. Its details open on the left.

steps.title: Acquiring a concept
steps.body: Keep going. A 1991 model — "A modular neural network model of concept acquisition" — asks how a network LEARNS a new concept at all. We've stayed inside one idea the whole way: how a network comes to know. This is where it gets strange.

steps.title: Two wormholes
steps.body: Not every link is a citation. Two violet conduits bloom from this node — semantic kin in other fields. One reaches another network model of how minds form categories. The other leaps clean out of machine learning. Follow the far one.

steps.title: Plato's problem
steps.body: The humanities. "A solution to Plato's problem" (1997) — latent semantic analysis. It answers a question Plato asked 2,400 years ago: how do we come to know so much from so little? The same idea — meaning learned from patterns of co-occurrence — that a neural network uses to acquire a concept. A model of mind, alive in philosophy, and the citation graph would never have shown you.

steps.title: Through the wormhole
steps.body: Cross the semantic edge into the humanities — the jump no citation chain ever offered. Kinship the graph found by meaning, not by reference.

steps.title: Found by exploring
steps.body: We started on connectionism, followed it through semantic cognition and concept learning, and a wormhole leapt clean out of machine learning into a 2,400-year-old question in philosophy. That's the point — you don't search for the surprise, you explore until it finds you.
